items.title: Mechanical vs functional classification
items.contentHtml:
Classification

  • Mechanical obstruction: physical obstacle to intestinal transit, it can be simple (without vascular compromise) or strangled (with ischemia).
  • Functional ileus (paralytic): absence of peristalsis without mechanical obstacle; causes: peritonitis, abdominal surgery, electrolyte disorders (hypokalemia), drugs (opioids).
  • Key fact: in paralytic ileus intestinal sounds are absent; In the initial mechanical obstruction there are metallic noises and hyperperistalsis.

items.foco: Most common causes of small intestine obstruction
items.title: ID Causes: flanges, hernias, tumors
items.contentHtml:
Causes of small intestine obstruction

  • Bands or adhesions (60-70%): history of previous abdominal surgery.
  • External hernias (10-20%): inguinal, femoral, umbilical; entrapment of the loop.
  • Tumors (5-10%): primary or metastatic, especially adenocarcinoma or carcinoid tumor.
  • Other: Crohn's disease (inflammatory stricture), volvulus of the small intestine, ileus biliary .

items.foco: Most common causes of large intestine obstruction
items.title: Causes of colon obstruction
items.contentHtml:
Causes of large intestine obstruction

  • Colorectal cancer (60-70%): especially in left colon and sigmoid.
  • Sigmoid volvulus (10-15%): more common in the elderly, chronic constipation, megacolon.
  • Diverticulitis (inflammatory stricture) and fecal impaction.
  • Key fact: colonic obstruction usually presents with late vomiting and marked distention.

items.foco: Classic triad: pain, vomiting, distension, absence of bowel movements
items.title: Classic obstructive triad
items.contentHtml:
Classic triad of intestinal obstruction

  • Colic-like abdominal pain (periumbilical in ID, hypogastrium in colon) with exacerbations.
  • Vomiting: early in high obstruction (ID), later and fecaloid in colon.
  • Abdominal distension: greater in colon and distal obstruction of ID.
  • Absence of bowel movements and gases(complete obstruction); in partial obstruction there may be partial elimination.

items.foco: Radiological signs: air-fluid levels and dilated loops
items.title: Radiological signs in obstruction
items.contentHtml:
Radiological signs

  • Simple standing abdominal X-ray: stepped air-fluid levels in small bowel obstruction.
  • Dilated loops (>3 cm in ID, >6 cm in colon) proximal to the point of obstruction.
  • "stack of coins" sign (closely spaced circular plicae) in obstruction of ID.
  • In colon obstruction: prominent haustra, absence of distal gas.

items.title: Sigmoid volvulus and coffee bean sign
items.contentHtml:
Sigmoid volvulus

  • Coffee bean sign in simple abdominal X-ray: dilated sigmoid loop in the shape of an inverted "U" with two adjacent loops and a dense central line (edematous walls).
  • Presentation: elderly, chronic constipation, severe abdominal distension, pain.
  • Complication: strangulation the loop, ischemia and perforation.
  • Initial treatment: endoscopic decompression (colonoscopy) with placement of a rectal probe; if it fails or there are signs of ischemia, surgery.

items.foco: Gallstone ileus: Rigler's sign (pneumobilia and obstruction)
items.title: Gallstone ileus and Rigler's sign
items.contentHtml:
Gallory ileus

  • Rigler's sign (triad): pneumobilia (air in the bile duct), intestinal obstruction (air-fluid levels) and ectopic gallstone visible in the pelvis or in the obstructed loop.
  • Pathophysiology: cholecystoenteric fistula (chronic cholecystitis) that allows stones to pass into the duodenum or jejunum, impacting the ileocecal valve (most common site).
  • Treatment: enterolithotomy (extraction of the stone) and fistula repair if possible.

items.foco: Pyloric stenosis: double bubble sign in newborn
items.title: Pyloric stenosis and double bubble
items.contentHtml:
Hypertrophic pyloric stenosis of the infant

  • Double bubble sign in simple abdominal X-ray: two gas bubbles (stomach and proximal duodenum) with absence of distal gas, indicating proximal duodenal obstruction.
  • Pyloric stenosis: hypertrophy of the pylorus (circular muscle) that causes obstruction of the gastric outlet; It is not a typical intestinal obstruction but is included in the differential diagnosis of vomiting in newborns.
  • Clinic: non-bilious projectile vomiting, visible peristaltic wave and palpable pyloric olive.

items.foco: Differential diagnosis: paralytic ileus vs mechanical obstruction
items.title: DDx paralytic ileus vs mechanical obstruction
items.contentHtml:
Differential diagnosis

  • Paralytic ileus: absence of bowel sounds, mild diffuse pain, generalized distention, X-ray with gas throughout the intestine (without typical air-fluid levels), history of surgery or peritonitis.
  • Mechanical obstruction: hyperactive sounds with borborygmi, colicky pain, staggered air-fluid levels, dilated loop proximal to the point of obstruction.
  • Neostigmine test (0.5-2 mg IV) to differentiate: if there is a response (elimination of gases) it suggests paralytic ileus; In mechanical obstruction there is no response or it worsens.

items.foco: Initial treatment: nasogastric tube, fluids, fasting
items.title: Conservative initial treatment
items.contentHtml:
Initial treatment

  • Fasting (NPO) for intestinal rest.
  • Nasogastric tube (NGT) by gravity or intermittent aspiration for gastric decompression and relief of vomiting.
  • Replacement of fluids and electrolytes: isotonic solution (lactated Ringer's or NaCl 0.9%) with correction of hypokalemia (risk of ileus).
  • Monitoring: diuresis, electrolytes, renal function; consider bladder catheter if instability.
  • In partial obstruction without warning signs, conservative management can be attempted for 24-48 hours.

items.title: Surgical indications
items.contentHtml:
Surgical indications

  • Strangulation (vascular compromise): continuous pain, fever, leukocytosis, metabolic acidosis, lactate elevation.
  • Ischemia or perforation (signs of peritoneal irritation: rigidity, rebound, defense).
  • Failure of the conservative treatment (persistence or worsening after 24-48 hours).
  • Complete colonic obstruction (risk of perforation due to distension) usually requires urgent surgery.
  • In sigmoid volvulus without ischemia: initial endoscopic decompression; if there are signs of ischemia or perforation, laparotomy.

items.title: Serious complications
items.contentHtml:
Complications

  • Intestinal perforation: passage of intestinal contents into the peritoneal cavity, causing peritonitis (severe pain, stiffness, rebound, fever).
  • Sepsis: systemic response to infection, tachycardia, hypotension, lactic acidosis, organ dysfunction.
  • Risk factors: prolonged obstruction, ischemia, strangulation, immunosuppression. Management: urgent surgery (resection of the necrotic/perforated loop), broad-spectrum antibiotics (covering gram-negative and anaerobes: piperacillin-tazobactam or cefepime + metronidazole), hemodynamic support.
